section.title: The Portions for the Priests
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the entire first collection of sacrificial law. It gives the law of the guilt offering, adds detailed rules for the peace offering's thanksgiving, vow, and freewill forms, forbids eating fat or blood, and finally states what belongs to the priests, closing with a summary of all five offerings given at Sinai.
section.thesis: God closes the sacrificial law by detailing the guilt offering's procedure, the timing rules for eating the peace offering, the permanent ban on eating fat and blood, and the priestly portions, the breast and thigh, teaching that those who serve at the altar are rightly provided for by the people they serve.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the law of the guilt offering; the law of the peace offering and its timing for thanksgiving, vow, and freewill forms; the ban on fat and blood; and the priestly portions with the chapter's closing summary.

units.label: The law of the guilt offering
units.note: most holy, belonging to the priest

verses.text: “Now this is the law of the guilt offering, which is most holy:
The guilt offering must be slaughtered in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, and the priest shall sprinkle its blood on all sides of the altar.
And all the fat from it shall be offered: the fat tail, the fat that covers the entrails,
both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which is to be removed with the kidneys.
The priest shall burn them on the altar as an offering made by fire to the LORD; it is a guilt offering.
Every male among the priests may eat of it. It must be eaten in a holy place; it is most holy.
The guilt offering is like the sin offering; the same law applies to both. It belongs to the priest who makes atonement with it.
As for the priest who presents a burnt offering for anyone, the hide of that offering belongs to him.
Likewise, every grain offering that is baked in an oven or cooked in a pan or on a griddle belongs to the priest who presents it,
and every grain offering, whether dry or mixed with oil, belongs equally to all the sons of Aaron.

units.label: The law of the peace offering

verses.text: Now this is the law of the peace offering that one may present to the LORD:

If he offers it in {{todah|thanksgiving}}, then along with the sacrifice of thanksgiving he shall offer unleavened cakes mixed with olive oil, unleavened wafers coated with oil, and well-kneaded cakes of fine flour mixed with oil.
Along with his peace offering of thanksgiving he is to present an offering with cakes of leavened bread.
From the cakes he must present one portion of each offering as a contribution to the LORD. It belongs to the priest who sprinkles the blood of the peace offering.
The meat of the sacrifice of his peace offering of thanksgiving must be eaten on the day he offers it; none of it may be left until morning.
If, however, the sacrifice he offers is a vow or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day he presents his sacrifice, but the remainder may be eaten on the next day.
But any meat of the sacrifice remaining until the third day must be burned up.

If any of the meat from his peace offering is eaten on the third day, it will not be accepted. It will not be credited to the one who presented it; it shall be an abomination, and the one who eats of it shall bear his iniquity.
Meat that touches anything unclean must not be eaten; it is to be burned up. As for any other meat, anyone who is ceremonially clean may eat it.
But if anyone who is unclean eats meat from the peace offering that belongs to the LORD, that person must be cut off from his people.

If one touches anything unclean, whether human uncleanness, an unclean animal, or any unclean, detestable thing, and then eats any of the meat of the peace offering that belongs to the LORD, that person must be cut off from his people.”
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say, ‘You are not to eat any of the fat of an ox, a sheep, or a goat.
The fat of an animal found dead or mauled by wild beasts may be used for any other purpose, but you must not eat it.
If anyone eats the fat of an animal from which an offering made by fire may be presented to the LORD, the one who eats it must be cut off from his people.
You must not eat the blood of any bird or animal in any of your dwellings.
If anyone eats blood, that person must be cut off from his people.’”
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say, ‘Anyone who presents a peace offering to the LORD must bring it as his sacrifice to the LORD.
With his own hands he is to bring the offerings made by fire to the LORD; he shall bring the fat, together with the breast, and {{tenufah|wave the breast}} as a wave offering before the LORD.
The priest is to burn the fat on the altar, but the breast belongs to Aaron and his sons.
And you are to give the right thigh to the priest as a {{terumah|contribution}} from your peace offering.
The son of Aaron who presents the blood and fat of the peace offering shall have the right thigh as a portion.

I have taken from the sons of Israel the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution of their peace offerings, and I have given them to Aaron the priest and his sons as a permanent portion from the sons of Israel.’”
This is the portion of the offerings made by fire to the LORD for Aaron and his sons since the day they were presented to serve the LORD as priests.
On the day they were anointed, the LORD commanded that this be given them by the sons of Israel. It is a permanent portion for the generations to come.
This is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the peace offering,
which the LORD gave Moses on Mount Sinai on the day He commanded the Israelites to present their offerings to the LORD in the Wilderness of Sinai.

todah.gloss: Thanksgiving, one specific occasion for the peace offering, given in response to a specific answered prayer or deliverance. Its meat had to be eaten the same day, so that gratitude was expressed and shared immediately, not delayed or hoarded.

tenufah.gloss: A wave offering, presented with a horizontal waving motion before the LORD, symbolically presenting the portion to God before it is given to the priest who serves at the altar.

terumah.gloss: A contribution or heave offering, lifted up and set apart as a gift dedicated to the LORD, then given as the rightful, permanent portion of the priests who minister on Israel's behalf.

basic.context: This long chapter closes the entire opening collection of sacrificial law. It details the guilt offering's procedure, adds rules for the peace offering's thanksgiving, vow, and freewill forms, and their strict timing for eating the meat, permanently forbids eating fat or blood, and finally specifies that the breast and thigh of the peace offering belong to the priests who serve at the altar.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes actual old covenant ritual law and priestly provision for Israel; it is not a code Christians observe today. Its abiding truth is that those who serve God's people at the altar deserve to be supported by those they serve, and that gratitude to God, pictured in the thanksgiving offering, calls for prompt, shared celebration rather than delay.
basic.originalLanguages: The todah, thanksgiving offering, had to be eaten the same day, teaching that gratitude is meant to be expressed immediately and shared, not hoarded. The tenufah, wave offering, and terumah, contribution, describe the breast and thigh set apart and lifted up as the priests' permanent portion for their service at the altar.
basic.typology: The requirement that thanksgiving offerings be eaten the same day, shared in community rather than kept back, anticipates the immediate, communal joy of the Lord's Supper, where believers give thanks, eucharisteo, together. The priest's rightful portion from the people's offerings anticipates Paul's teaching that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel, 1 Corinthians 9:14.
basic.christ: Christ is the true and greater priest, whose provision does not come from the people's offerings but who Himself is the offering, giving His body and blood so that His people might partake, echoing the priestly meal that pictured shared fellowship through sacrifice, 1 Corinthians 10:16-17.
basic.god: God ensures that those who mediate atonement, the priests, are provided for by the very people they serve, showing His concern for those in ministry not to be left destitute. He also insists that thanksgiving be expressed promptly and shared, not hoarded privately, revealing His desire for gratitude that spills outward into community.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 9:13-14 argues that those who serve at the altar share in what is offered, just as the Lord commanded those who preach the gospel to receive their living from it. 1 Corinthians 10:16-17 speaks of sharing one bread together as participation in Christ. Hebrews 13:15 calls believers to offer a sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips that confess His name.
basic.oneStory: This closing chapter of the sacrificial law provides for the priests who serve at the altar and calls Israel to prompt, shared thanksgiving rather than delayed or private gratitude. It anticipates the New Testament pattern of supporting gospel ministers and gathering to give thanks together in Christ, our true peace offering, at His table.

context.literary: This chapter closes the entire block of sacrificial instruction begun in chapter one, summarized explicitly in its final verses, this is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the peace offering, given at Sinai.
context.historical: Israel's priests owned no tribal land inheritance, Numbers 18:20, depending instead on these very portions from the people's offerings for their livelihood, a system that tied the priesthood's daily bread directly to the people's worship.
context.canonical: The principle that those who serve at the altar share in the offering becomes foundational for Paul's argument that gospel workers deserve support from those they serve, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, directly applying this Levitical pattern to the New Testament church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To conclude the sacrificial law by detailing the guilt and peace offerings, permanently forbidding the eating of fat and blood, and establishing the priests' rightful portion from the peace offerings as the provision for their service at the altar.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes specific old covenant ritual and provision rules for Israel's priesthood; it is not a binding pattern for church practice today. Its abiding principle is that those who serve God's people in ministry deserve support, and that gratitude to God calls for prompt, communal expression.

debates.question: Why must the thanksgiving peace offering be eaten the same day, while a vow or freewill offering may be eaten over two days, verses 15 to 16?

views.label: Thanksgiving calls for immediate, spontaneous celebration
views.case: A todah offering responds to a specific answered prayer, so its meat is to be consumed quickly, matching the immediacy of the gratitude being expressed, likely requiring the worshiper to gather others quickly to share the meal.

views.label: A graduated scale distinguishing spontaneous from planned offerings
views.case: Others note that vow and freewill offerings were often planned further in advance, so a longer window for eating them may simply reflect practical differences in how these offerings were normally brought and consumed.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the specific timing rules reinforced eating sacrificial meat while it was fresh and within community, never letting it become an occasion for decay, waste, or careless disregard for what was holy.

study.originalLanguages: The todah, thanksgiving offering, required immediate, shared eating the same day, teaching that gratitude to God is meant to be expressed promptly and openly. The tenufah, wave offering, and terumah, contribution, name the horizontal waving and the lifted portion that set apart the breast and thigh as the permanent, rightful share of the priests who ministered at the altar.

typology.type: The thanksgiving offering eaten the same day, shared with others
typology.fulfillment: The immediate, communal thanksgiving believers now share at the Lord's Table, Luke 22:19.

typology.type: The priest's rightful portion from the people's offerings
typology.fulfillment: Gospel ministers rightly supported by those they serve, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14.

study.christ: Paul draws directly on this chapter's logic in 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, arguing that just as those who served at the altar shared in what was offered, so those who preach the gospel should receive their living from it. More deeply, Christ Himself becomes the true offering His people partake of, His body and blood given so that believers might share, koinonia, in Him at His table, 1 Corinthians 10:16-17.
study.god: God's law provides concretely for those who serve Him at the altar, showing His concern that ministry not be left unsupported by those it serves. His requirement that thanksgiving offerings be eaten promptly, in community, reveals a God who desires gratitude expressed openly and shared, not hoarded in private religious observance.

crossReferences.note: Those who serve at the altar share in the offerings, just as the Lord commanded that gospel preachers receive their living from the gospel.
Sharing the one bread and cup is a participation in Christ, echoing the shared sacrificial meal of the peace offering.
Believers are called to continually offer a sacrifice of praise to God, the fruit of lips that confess His name.

study.oneStory: This chapter closes the sacrificial law by providing for the priests who mediate atonement and by calling Israel to prompt, shared thanksgiving rather than private or delayed gratitude. It anticipates both the New Testament pattern of supporting those who serve in gospel ministry and the shared fellowship believers now enjoy at the table of Christ, our true peace and thanksgiving offering.

quiz.q: How quickly must the meat of a thanksgiving peace offering be eaten?

options.text: On the same day it is offered; none may be left until morning.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly requires this same day timing for the thanksgiving offering.

options.text: Within seven days.
options.note: The thanksgiving offering specifically requires same day consumption; a vow or freewill offering allows two days, not seven.

options.text: It may be kept indefinitely as long as it is refrigerated.
options.note: The text gives a strict same day requirement, with no allowance for extended storage.

options.text: No timing restriction is given.
options.note: Verse 15 gives an explicit, strict timing requirement.

quiz.q: What happens to meat from a peace offering that is eaten on the third day?

options.text: It is not accepted, will not be credited to the offerer, and is considered an abomination.
options.note: Verse 18 states this consequence explicitly for meat eaten on the third day.

options.text: It is considered especially holy and reserved for the high priest alone.
options.note: The text describes the opposite, that third day meat becomes unacceptable and defiling.

options.text: Nothing changes; it remains acceptable to eat at any time.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly states this meat becomes unacceptable after the permitted time.

options.text: No consequence is given.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly states the consequence.

quiz.q: What two things does this chapter permanently forbid Israel from eating?

options.text: Fat and blood.
options.note: Verses 23 to 27 establish this permanent prohibition against eating fat or blood of any bird or animal.

options.text: Bread and wine.
options.note: Bread and wine are not the subjects of this prohibition; fat and blood are.

options.text: Fish and honey.
options.note: The prohibition named in this chapter concerns fat and blood, not fish or honey.

options.text: Nothing is forbidden here.
options.note: Verses 23 to 27 give an explicit, permanent prohibition.

quiz.q: Which two portions of the peace offering are given permanently to Aaron and his sons?

options.text: The breast, as a wave offering, and the right thigh, as a contribution.
options.note: Verses 30 to 34 specify these exact portions as the priests' permanent share.

options.text: The entire animal, with nothing left for the worshiper.
options.note: The peace offering's meat is largely eaten by the worshiper; only the breast and thigh belong permanently to the priests.

options.text: The hide and the head only.
options.note: The hide belongs to the priest specifically for burnt offerings, verse 8; the peace offering's priestly portion is the breast and thigh.

options.text: No specific portion is given to the priests.
options.note: Verses 30 to 34 give a specific, detailed portion to the priests.

quiz.q: What does the chapter's closing summary, verses 37 to 38, list as the offerings given to Moses at Sinai?

options.text: The burnt offering, grain offering, sin offering, guilt offering, ordination offering, and peace offering.
options.note: Verse 37 lists exactly these six categories as a summary of the sacrificial law just given.

options.text: Only the burnt offering and the grain offering.
options.note: Verse 37 lists six distinct categories, not merely two.

options.text: The Ten Commandments.
options.note: This chapter's summary concerns the sacrificial offerings, not the Decalogue, which was given earlier in Exodus 20.

options.text: No summary is given.
options.note: Verses 37 to 38 give an explicit summary list.

questions: The thanksgiving offering had to be eaten the same day, shared quickly rather than hoarded. When God answers a specific prayer in your life, do you celebrate that promptly and share it with others, or do you let gratitude quietly fade?

The priests were given a rightful portion from the people's offerings so they would not go without. Do you support those who serve you spiritually, whether pastors, teachers, or leaders, as generously as this law required?
Fat and blood were permanently off limits, reserved wholly for God. What is the equivalent in your life, something you know belongs to God alone but you are tempted to keep or use for yourself?

Meat eaten past its proper time became an abomination rather than a blessing. Where might something good in your life, kept past its right season or used the wrong way, have turned from blessing into harm?
This chapter closes the entire sacrificial law given at Sinai. As you look back over these first offerings, which one has spoken most directly to where you are with God right now, and why?

translationNotes.note: Thanksgiving, todah, describes an offering brought in response to a specific deliverance or answered prayer, distinct from a general vow, neder, or freewill offering, nedavah, brought without a specific preceding occasion.

It shall be an abomination translates a strong Hebrew term, piggul, used specifically of sacrificial meat eaten past its permitted time, showing that even a holy offering could become defiling if handled wrongly.

The wave offering, tenufah, and the contribution, terumah, describe distinct ritual gestures, a horizontal waving and a lifting up, both marking the breast and thigh as formally set apart for the priesthood before being eaten.
